common_name: Pampas Grass (South American Giant Ornamental Plume Grass)

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as new growth emerges, or plant container-grown plants in spring after last frost; pampas grass is slow to establish in its first year and then grows rapidly — mature clumps reach full ornamental size (6–10 feet) by year 2–3. Plant in a permanent location with full sun and excellent drainage; the sharp leaf blades require leather gloves when dividing or handling. In zone 9, pampas grass is evergreen to semi-evergreen and rarely needs division for 5–10 years.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; once established, pampas grass is extremely drought-tolerant and requires virtually no supplemental irrigation except during establishment. Pampas grass (Cortaderia selloana) is one of the most dramatic and instantly recognizable ornamental grasses in cultivation — a native of the pampas (grasslands) of Argentina, Brazil, and Chile that forms enormous clumps of arching, sharp-edged, blue-green leaves 4–6 feet tall topped by tall stalks bearing spectacular, feathery, silky white to cream (female) or more narrow silver (male) plumes that emerge in late summer and persist through fall and winter, providing exceptional dried-flower display for 4–6 months. Female plants produce the larger, showier plumes preferred in ornamental use; most commercial plants are female. The plumes, which can reach 12 feet in total height, are outstanding as fresh-cut or dried arrangements and are long-lasting on the plant through winter. Pampas grass is so visually dominant that a single specimen becomes a landscape focal point — the feathery white plumes blowing in late-summer breezes have become iconic in coastal and tropical garden landscapes. In Gulf Coast zone 8–9 conditions, pampas grass is evergreen to semi-evergreen, forming an expanding clump that may need division every 5–7 years as the center dies out. Plants are best divided with a sharp spade or mattock; the razor-sharp leaf margins make leather gloves and long sleeves essential for any hands-on work. Note: pampas grass has become invasive in coastal California and New Zealand and should not be planted in fire-prone areas or where it may escape into natural areas.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Grasses for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Grasses
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Ornamental Grasses for Louisiana

common_name: Tropicanna Canna (Phasion Canna, Striped-Foliage Tropical Canna)

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring and plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil when soil temperatures are above 60°F; each division should have at least one eye (growing point). In zone 9, established clumps overwinter in ground and regrow vigorously each spring — established plants put up new shoots from March onward. In zone 7–8, lift rhizomes after frost kills the foliage, allow to dry, and store frost-free over winter.

directions: Plant rhizomes 3–4 inches deep in rich, consistently moist, well-amended soil in full sun; feed with a balanced fertilizer monthly during the growing season for maximum foliage color and flowering. Tropicanna (also sold under the European trademark name 'Phasion') is the most widely grown ornamental canna in the world and arguably the most visually spectacular — selected specifically for its extraordinary foliage, which is striped in vivid bronze, green, gold, pink, and red bands running the length of each paddle-shaped leaf, creating a tropical stained-glass effect unlike any other common garden plant. The foliage alone is bold enough to justify growing Tropicanna as a pure foliage plant, but the plants also produce vibrant bright orange flowers on tall stalks above the foliage from summer through fall, adding another layer of tropical color. Each individual plant grows to 4–6 feet tall with an equally bold spread; in mass plantings or as specimen plants in large containers, Tropicanna creates instant tropical impact that takes most ornamentals years to achieve. The foliage color is most intense in full sun — shaded plants develop a solid green that loses most of the characteristic striping. In zone 9, Tropicanna overwinters reliably in the ground and expands by rhizome division each year, requiring periodic thinning to prevent overcrowding. Provide consistent moisture — cannas are heavy feeders and drinkers — but excellent drainage is equally critical, as standing water causes rhizome rot.
sources: American Canna Society — Tropicanna variety history and cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Canna Growing Guide
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Plants for Gulf South Gardens

common_name: Winged Passion Fruit (Fragrant Granadilla, Sweet Calabash, Red Passion Fruit)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings after last frost in spring, providing a strong trellis, fence, or arbor for the vigorous vines; Passiflora alata climbs by tendrils and can cover 15–20 feet of support within a single growing season. In zone 9–10, plants are perennial and develop a permanent woody framework; in zone 8b they may die to the ground in hard winters but often regenerate from roots. First fruit set typically occurs in the second season after planting.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil and provide a sturdy trellis or fence; established plants fruit more reliably than recently transplanted ones, so patience through the first season is required. Passiflora alata (winged passion fruit) is a Brazilian passion fruit species that stands out from the more commonly grown P. edulis for two extraordinary qualities: its flowers and its fruit fragrance. The flowers are among the most beautiful of any Passiflora — large (3–4 inches across), deeply colored in crimson-red sepals with purple-and-white banded corona filaments, strongly fragrant, and produced in profusion from summer through fall. The aroma alone makes this an exceptional garden plant. The large, oval to oblong fruits (3–5 inches long) are distinctly fragrant when ripe, with a sweet, aromatic, tropical scent often described as more complex and perfumed than standard purple passion fruit (P. edulis); the golden-orange interior pulp is sweet and aromatic with a flavor that is mellower and less acidic than standard passion fruit, more approachable for fresh eating. The vine grows vigorously in zone 9–10, covering trellises and fences rapidly with glossy dark green leaves that have distinctly winged (four-angled) petioles — the characteristic that gives the species its common name. Pollination by large bees (especially carpenter bees) or hand-pollination improves fruit set. One of the most rewarding edible passion fruits for Gulf Coast gardens where it performs as a true perennial.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Passionfruit Growing in Florida
USDA National Plant Germplasm System — Passiflora alata accessions
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Fruits for Louisiana

common_name: Princess Flower (Glory Bush, Purple Glory Tree, Brazilian Spider Flower)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), organic-matter-rich soil; Tibouchina strongly prefers well-drained soil and suffers from root rot in waterlogged conditions. In zone 9–10, plants are perennial shrubs that grow to 8–15 feet; they are frost-sensitive and benefit from a protected south- or east-facing microclimate in the cooler end of zone 9. Hard frosts kill the above-ground portion; plants often regenerate from the root crown in zone 9a.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic, organically rich soil; water consistently but ensure excellent drainage, as Tibouchina is intolerant of wet feet. Princess flower (Tibouchina urvilleana) is one of the most magnificent flowering shrubs for Gulf South and tropical gardens — a native of southern Brazil that produces enormous (3–4 inch), five-petaled, royal-purple flowers of a saturated, jewel-toned violet-purple color rarely found in any other woody plant. The flowers are borne singly or in small clusters at the branch tips continuously from summer through fall, with the heaviest bloom often in late summer and early fall; the large, soft, velvety leaves with their strongly ribbed texture add tactile and visual interest even when not in flower. Plants grow 4–15 feet depending on frost exposure and cultural conditions — in frost-free climates Tibouchina becomes a large shrub or small tree; in zone 9 where hard freezes occur, plants die back to the crown in cold winters and return as smaller shrubs each spring. The rich purple flower color pairs exceptionally well with golden or orange flowering plants, bougainvillea, and tropical foliage plants; Tibouchina is frequently used as a focal-point specimen shrub in Gulf Coast landscapes and performs well in large containers (12+ gallon) where it can be moved to frost protection in winter. Slightly acid soil (pH 5.5–6.5) with added organic matter is important for sustained health and heavy flowering — alkaline soil causes interveinal chlorosis.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tibouchina urvilleana (Glory Bush)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Shrubs for Texas
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Landscape Plants for the Gulf South

common_name: Firebush (Scarlet Bush, Hummingbird Bush, Mexican Firebush)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring after last frost; firebush establishes quickly and begins flowering within the first summer. In zone 9–10, firebush is perennial and grows to 6–12 feet, often dying back to the ground after hard freezes but regenerating vigorously from the root crown. In zone 8, it performs as an annual or a returning perennial depending on winter severity. Established plants require minimal supplemental irrigation except during severe drought.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil of any quality — firebush thrives in poor, sandy, low-fertility conditions and performs well without supplemental fertilization once established. Firebush (Hamelia patens) is one of the highest-value native flowering shrubs for wildlife-friendly Gulf Coast and Florida landscapes — a Florida native and tropical American species that produces tubular, bright orange-to-red flowers in dense, clustered cymes from early summer continuously through the first frost, creating one of the longest-lasting flowering displays of any shrub in the region. The relentless summer bloom makes firebush a magnet for hummingbirds, butterflies (especially Gulf fritillary, zebra longwing, and swallowtails), and native bees throughout the growing season; the small, dark purple-black berries that follow the flowers are eaten by mockingbirds, cardinals, and other berry-eating birds, making firebush valuable at multiple levels of the food web. Unlike many nectar plants, firebush blooms heavily in partial shade as well as full sun, making it one of the few flowering shrubs that reliably attracts hummingbirds in partially shaded sites. The foliage is medium green to bronze-tinged (more colorful cultivars like 'Compacta' exist) with a whorled leaf arrangement that gives the plant a distinctive tropical texture. Firebush is extremely heat and drought tolerant once established, grows rapidly in poor soils, and requires essentially no care beyond occasional pruning to shape — an ideal low-maintenance native for zone 9 wildlife gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hamelia patens, Firebush
University of Florida Center for Landscape Conservation and Ecology — Native Plant Guide
